request.question: What was the record for the head football coach of this private four-year Christian-based college founded in Salina, Kansas, United States?

title: Tim McCarty
Kansas Wesleyan University
Saint Joseph Seminary College
Willie Fritz
Ron Dupree
C. L. Williams
Coach Miller
Mark Raymond
Carl Anderson (American football)
Tom Keele
sentences: Tim McCarty is an American football coach and former player.
He is currently the head football coach at East Central University in Ada, Oklahoma, a position he first held from 2004 to 2005 and then resume in 2009.
From 1999 to 2003 McCarty was the head football coach at Tabor College in Hillsboro, Kansas.
His two tenures at East Central were sepeated by a three-year stint at the assistant head football coach at Kansas State University.
Kansas Wesleyan University is a private four-year Christian-based college founded in Salina, Kansas, United States in 1886.
It is affiliated with the United Methodist Church.
About 850 students attend the 28 acre campus.

The university is accredited by the North Central Association of Colleges and Schools, the National League for Nursing, the National Council for Accreditation of Teacher Education (NCATE), and the Kansas State Department of Education.
Saint Joseph Seminary College, also known as St. Ben or St. Ben's, is a private four-year seminary college founded in 1891.
It is operated by the Benedictine Monks of Saint Joseph Abbey and the dioceses of the ecclesiastical provinces of New Orleans and Mobile, Alabama.
The 1200 acre piney woods campus is located in St. Benedict, Louisiana, north of Covington.
Willie Fritz (born April 2, 1960) is an American football coach and former player.
He is the current head coach at Tulane University.
From 2014 to 2015, he was head coach at Georgia Southern University.
From 2010 to 2013, he was the head football coach at Sam Houston State University.
From 1997 to 2009, Fritz served as the head football coach at the University of Central Missouri.
From 1993 to 1996, he was the head football coach at Blinn College, a junior college in Brenham, Texas.
Ron Dupree is a former American football coach.
Dupree was the 15th head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for three seasons, from 1979 to 1980 and again in 1996.
His coaching record at Kansas Wesleyan was 11–18.
C. L. Williams was an American football coach.
He was the third head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for two seasons, from 1908 to 1909, compiling a record of 9–5.
Coach Miller was an American football coach.
He was the second head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for one season, in 1906, and compiling a record of 0–1.
Mark Raymond is an American football coach, currently serving as the head football coach at Williams College.
Raymond served as the head football coach at St. Lawrence University from 2010 to 2015, winning two conference titles and compiling an overall record of 31–30.
Raymond was named Liberty League Coach of the Year following the conclusion of both the 2010 and 2015 seasons at St. Lawrence.
Raymond was named head football coach at Williams College on February 24, 2016.
Carl Rudolph Frederick "Swede" Anderson IV (September 9, 1898 – April 30, 1978) was an American college football coach at Western Kentucky University and Howard Payne University.
Anderson graduated from Centre College in Danville, Kentucky in 1924, where he played in the backfield with legendary alumnus Bo McMillin.
Anderson then followed McMillin to Centenary College of Louisiana and Geneva College.
Anderson then served one year as the head football coach at Western Kentucky, before moving to Kansas State as its freshman team coach in 1930.
Anderson returned to Western Kentucky as its head coach from 1934 to 1937.
He was the backfield coach under McMillin at Indiana from 1938 to 1945.
He then returned to his alma mater, Centre College, where he coached the Praying Colonels until 1950.
The following season, Anderson became the seventh head football coach at the Howard Payne University in Brownwood, Texas and held that position from 1951 to 1952.
His coaching record at Howard Payne was 7–10.
Tom Keele (born c. 1933) is a former American football coach.
He served as the head football coach at California State University, Northridge from 1979 to 1985, compiling a record of 31–42–1.
Keele graduated from Jefferson High School in Portland Oregon in 1951.
He attended the University of Oregon, where he played football for the Oregon Webfoots as a tackle from 1957 to 1959.
Keele began his coaching career in 1960 at North Eugene High School in Eugene, Oregon, working two years as an assistant football coach and sophomore basketball coach.
He moved to Oregon City High School in Oregon City, Oregon in 1962, serving as head football coach and leading his team to a 9–1–1 record.
The following year, he was hired as head football coach at the newly-formed Sheldon High School in Eugene.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What was the record for the head football coach of this private four-year Christian-based college founded in Salina, Kansas, United States?
hop1_docs: Kansas Wesleyan University | Kansas Wesleyan University is a private four-year Christian-based college founded in Salina, Kansas, United States in 1886.  It is affiliated with the United Methodist Church.  About 850 students attend the 28 acre campus.  The university is accredited by the North Central Association of Colleges and Schools, the National League for Nursing, the National Council for Accreditation of Teacher Education (NCATE), and the Kansas State Department of Education.
Bethel College (Kansas) | Bethel College is a four-year private Christian liberal arts college in North Newton, Kansas, United States.  It is affiliated with the Mennonite Church USA.

Coach Miller | Coach Miller was an American football coach.  He was the second head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for one season, in 1906, and compiling a record of 0–1.

1908 Kansas State Aggies football team | The 1908 Kansas State Aggies football team represented Kansas State Agricultural College (now Kansas State University) in the 1908 college football season.  In their fourth year under head coach Mike Ahearn, the Aggies compiled a 6–2 record, and outscored their opponents by a combined total of 182 to 74.

1909 Kansas State Aggies football team | The 1909 Kansas State Aggies football team represented Kansas State Agricultural College (now Kansas State University) in the 1909 college football season.  In their fifth year under head coach Mike Ahearn, the Aggies compiled a 7–2 record, and outscored their opponents by a combined total of 320 to 11.

Matt Drinkall | Matt Drinkall is an American football coach.  In January 2014 he was hired as the head coach of the Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes in Salina, Kansas.  He has worked previously as a football coach at the high school and college level.

W. F. Ragle | W. F. Ragle was an American football coach.  He was the fifth head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for one season, in 1915 and compiling a record of 5–4.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "Kansas Wesleyan University" — [1]: "founded in Salina, Kansas, United States in 1886."
- "Coach Miller" — [3]: "second head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University" and "compiling a record of 0–1."
- "W. F. Ragle" — [7]: "fifth head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University" and "compiling a record of 5–4."
- "Matt Drinkall" — [6]: "In January 2014 he was hired as the head coach"

Ambiguities:
- "the head football coach" is ambiguous: passages list multiple coaches (Coach Miller, W. F. Ragle, Matt Drinkall).
- "Coach Miller" lacks a full personal name/identifier in the passages.
- Matt Drinkall's record is not provided in the passages (only hiring date).

Bridge / missing fact:
- Which specific head coach does the question refer to (name and season/tenure) is not specified.
- The definitive coach-record pairing needed to answer (the requested coach's win–loss record) is not uniquely established for "the head football coach" in these passages.

Retrieval direction:
- Open "Kansas Wesleyan University" (page) for context and possible list of head coaches.
- Open "Matt Drinkall" (page) to find his coaching record at Kansas Wesleyan.
- Open "Coach Miller" (page) and "W. F. Ragle" (page) to confirm records "0–1" and "5–4" and any full names/tenures.
- Search query: "Kansas Wesleyan head football coaches list" or "Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes football head coaches record"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
response.hop2_query: Kansas Wesleyan head football coach record
hop2_docs: Ron Dupree | Ron Dupree is a former American football coach.  Dupree was the 15th head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for three seasons, from 1979 to 1980 and again in 1996.  His coaching record at Kansas Wesleyan was 11–18.
List of Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes head football coaches | List of Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes head football coaches

Coach Miller | Coach Miller was an American football coach.  He was the second head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for one season, in 1906, and compiling a record of 0–1.

W. F. Ragle | W. F. Ragle was an American football coach.  He was the fifth head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for one season, in 1915 and compiling a record of 5–4.

C. L. Williams | C. L. Williams was an American football coach.  He was the third head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for two seasons, from 1908 to 1909, compiling a record of 9–5.

Zora G. Clevenger | Zora G. Clevenger (December 12, 1881 – November 24, 1970) was an American football, basketball, and baseball player, coach, and pioneering athletic director.  He served as the head football coach at Nebraska Wesleyan University (1908–1910), the University of Tennessee (1911–1915), and Kansas State University (1916–1919), compiling a record of 47–32–7.  Clevenger was also the head basketball coach at Indiana University (1904–1906), Nebraska Wesleyan (1907–1911), Tennessee (1911–1916), and Kansas State (1916–1919), and was baseball coach at Indiana (1905–1906), Nebraska Wesleyan (1908–1911), Tennessee (1911–1916), and Kansas State (1919–1921).  Clevenger served as the athletic director at Kansas State (1916–1920), the University of Missouri (1921–1923), and Indiana (1923–1946).  He was inducted into the College Football Hall of Fame as a player in 1968.

George R. Edwards | George R. Edwards was an American football and basketball coach.  He was the fourth head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for two seasons, in 1914 and again in 1917, and compiling a record of 4–11.
hop2_titles: List of Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes head football coaches
